title: Allen Choi
text:
Hello! Nice to meet you!
My name is Allen Choi, and I am currently a second-year student here at California Polytechnic State University - San Luis Obispo. I like to spend my time reading, learning more about statistics, and drinking boba!

title: Hass Avocado Analysis
text:
2 Data


Code
# Data obtained from https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/neuromusic/avocado-prices
avocados <- read_csv(here::here("portfolio_files", "Data", "avocado.csv"), 
                     show_col_types = FALSE)


The dataset contains 18249 observations of retailers selling avocados. More specifically, it contains the type of Hass avocado, average price of each avocado sold, what type of Hass avocado it is, and how large the avocado was.
Cleaning data


Code
avocados <- avocados |> 
  rename("Small" = "4046", "Large" = "4225", "ExtraLarge" = "4770") |> 
  filter(region != "TotalUS")




Code
regionsdata <- tibble(
  region = c("West", "Southeast", "SouthCentral", "Plains", "Northeast", 
           "Midsouth", "GreatLakes", "NorthernNewEngland", "WestTexNewMexico"))
statesdata <- tibble(
  region = c("California", "NewYork", "SouthCarolina"))
region_avocado <- avocados |> 
  semi_join(y = regionsdata, by = "region")

metro_avocado <- avocados |> 
  anti_join(y = statesdata, by = "region") |> 
  anti_join(y = regionsdata, by = "region")


Most organic small avocados:


Code
region_avocado |> 
  filter(type == "organic",
         year == 2017) |> 
  group_by(region) |> 
  mutate(most = max(Small)) |> 
  distinct(region, most) |> 
  arrange(desc(most)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  slice_head()


# A tibble: 1 √Ч 2
  region       most
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 Northeast 127052.


The Northeast region sold the most organic, small Hass avocados in 2017.
Most avocado sales by month


Code
avocados |> 
  separate(col = Date, into = c("Year", "Month", "Day"), sep = "-") |> 
  select(Month, AveragePrice:type) |> 
  group_by(Month) |> 
  mutate(most = max(`Total Volume`)) |> 
  distinct(Month, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  arrange(desc(most)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  slice_head()


# A tibble: 1 √Ч 12
  Month AveragePrвА¶¬є TotalвА¶¬≤ Small  Large ExtraвА¶¬≥ TotalвА¶вБі SmallвА¶вБµ LargeвА¶вБґ XLargвА¶вБЈ
  <chr>       <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1 02           1.07  45675. 1088. 35056.     151   9380.   9000.    379.       0
# вА¶ with 2 more variables: type <chr>, most <dbl>, and abbreviated variable
#   names ¬євАЛAveragePrice, ¬≤вАЛ`Total Volume`, ¬≥вАЛExtraLarge, вБівАЛ`Total Bags`,
#   вБµвАЛ`Small Bags`, вБґвАЛ`Large Bags`, вБЈвАЛ`XLarge Bags`


February is the month with the highest volume of avocado sales.
Top five metro areas for avocado volume


Code
top_five_metro <- metro_avocado |> 
  separate(col = Date, into = c("Year", "Month", "Day"), sep = "-") |> 
  select(region, AveragePrice:type) |> 
  group_by(region) |> 
  mutate(most = max(`Total Volume`)) |> 
  distinct(region, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  arrange(desc(most)) |> 
  select(region) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  slice_head(n = 5)
metro_avocado |> 
  semi_join(top_five_metro, by = "region") |> 
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = region, y = `Total Volume`)) +
  geom_boxplot()





Organic vs.¬†Conventional


Code
cal_region_data <- tibble(
  region = c("LosAngeles", "SanDiego", "Sacramento", "SanFrancisco"))
city_compare <- avocados |> 
  semi_join(cal_region_data) |> 
  group_by(region, type) |> 
  mutate(avg_price = mean(AveragePrice),
         median_price = median(AveragePrice),
         range_price = IQR(AveragePrice)) |> 
  distinct(region, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(region) |> 
  mutate(mean = (avg_price[type == "organic"] - 
                   avg_price[type == "conventional"]),
         median = (median_price[type == "organic"] - 
                     median_price[type == "conventional"])) |> 
  # https://stackoverflow.com/a/62958772
  distinct(region, mean, median) |> 
  ungroup()


Joining, by = "region"


Code
ggplot(data = city_compare, 
       mapping = aes(x = region, y = mean)) +
geom_col()





Code
print(city_compare)


# A tibble: 4 √Ч 3
  region        mean median
  <chr>        <dbl>  <dbl>
1 LosAngeles   0.479   0.45
2 Sacramento   0.695   0.75
3 SanDiego     0.673   0.68
4 SanFrancisco 0.814   0.91


San Francisco has the greatest change in average price between organic and conventional avocados. They have the highest mean average difference at 0.81 and highest median difference at 0.91.
Show the proportion of Hass avocado sales


Code
avocados |> 
  semi_join(cal_region_data) |> 
  select(Date, Small:ExtraLarge, type, region) |>
  group_by(region, type) |> 
  mutate(avg_num_s = mean(Small),
         avg_num_m = mean(Large),
         avg_num_l = mean(ExtraLarge)) |> 
  distinct(region, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  select(type:avg_num_l) |> 
  pivot_longer(avg_num_s:avg_num_l,
               names_to = "Size",
               values_to = "Averages") |> 
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = region, y = Averages, 
                       fill = factor(Size, levels = 
                                       c("avg_num_s", "avg_num_m", "avg_num_l")))) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity', #https://stackoverflow.com/a/59009108
           position = "fill") +
  labs(x = "Regions of CA", 
       y = "Proportion of Mean Avocados Sold", 
       fill = "Avocado Size",
       ) +
  facet_wrap(~ type) +
  scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2))


Joining, by = "region"

title: Hiphop Culture Study Analysis
text:
1 New dataset with only desired variables


Code
hiphop <- hiphop |> 
  mutate(ethnic = if_else(
    ethnic == "white", "White", "Non-White")) |> 
  select(subj, sex, ethnic, intl:unclassifiable) |> 
  distinct(subj, .keep_all = TRUE)




2 Male vs.¬†Female


Code
hiphop |> 
  group_by(sex) |> 
  summarize(across(intl:unclassifiable, mean)) |> 
  summarize(across(intl:unclassifiable, diff))


# A tibble: 1 √Ч 10
    intl vocal classical     folk  rock country    pop alternaвА¶¬є  hiphop unclaвА¶¬≤
   <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1 -0.190 0.688     0.189 -0.00955  1.13  -0.394 -0.201   -0.0568 -0.0945 -0.0206
# вА¶ with abbreviated variable names ¬євАЛalternative, ¬≤вАЛunclassifiable


Males were more familiar with artists in the vocal, classical, and rock genre, while females were more familiar with artists in the intl, folk, country, pop, alternative, hiphop, and unclassifiable genres.


3 White vs.¬†Non-White


Code
hiphop |> 
  group_by(ethnic) |> 
  summarize(across(intl:unclassifiable, mean)) |> 
  summarize(across(intl:unclassifiable, diff))


# A tibble: 1 √Ч 10
   intl vocal classical  folk  rock country     pop alternative hiphop unclassвА¶¬є
  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>       <dbl>  <dbl>     <dbl>
1 -1.59 0.526   -0.0485 0.270  1.51   0.490 -0.0431        1.19 -0.730   -0.0465
# вА¶ with abbreviated variable name ¬євАЛunclassifiable


Those who were white were more familiar with vocal, folk, rock, country, and alternative artists, while those who were non-white were more familiar with intl, classical, pop, hiphop, and unclassifiable.


4 Study Design Critique

How is the study supposed to conclude if musical tastes can вАЬpredict AAE vocabulary knowledge above and beyond ethnicityвАЭ when there was only 1 African-American in the study? There were also only 38 Non-White participants in the study.
All the participants in the study were enrolled in music classes at the University of Minnesota, so the music variables in the study would probably have heavily skewed responses.
Given how pop-culture knowledge and social network phenomena are generally known by those who are younger, and that the ages of the participants in the study are heavily skewed to the right, the data is probably not representative of those older than a certain age.
